title: The Role of Intuition in Healing
meta_description: Discover how your intuition is not a flaw but a powerful tool for healing. Learn to transform sensitivity into strength and unlock your innate gifts.
og_title: The Role of Intuition in Healing
og_description: Discover how your intuition is not a flaw but a powerful tool for healing. Learn to transform sensitivity into strength and unlock your innate gifts.
body:
You Were Never Too Sensitive

There's a moment that happens for almost everyone who walks through the door of this work. You're sitting there, maybe on a couch or at a kitchen table, and someone says something that lands like a punch to the chest. Not because it hurt - but because it was true. Something you've known your whole life but never had words for.

For me, that moment came when a teacher looked at me and said, "You don't have a boundary problem. You have a gift you don't know how to use yet."

I cried for about twenty minutes. Right?! Because I'd spent thirty years thinking something was broken in me. That I felt too much, knew too much, couldn't turn it off. That my sensitivity was a liability I had to manage instead of a capacity I could develop.

Here's what I know now, and what I want you to know: your intuition isn't separate from your healing. They're the same process moving in different directions. You can't heal without listening to what you already know. And you can't develop your intuition without letting it touch the places that hurt.

This isn't spiritual bypass. This isn't "just think positive and your wounds will disappear." That's garbage and you know it. Real healing requires you to get honest about what you feel, what you've always felt, and what you've been trained to ignore. Your intuition is the only part of you that never bought the lie that you had to shrink yourself to be safe.

"Your intuition is not the part of you that runs from pain. It's the part that knows how to walk through it without losing yourself."

The Wound That Taught You Not to Trust Yourself

Let's talk about where this starts. Because it didn't start with you choosing to ignore your gut. That was taught. Carefully. Repeatedly. By people who didn't know what they were doing.

Think about the first time you knew something was wrong but were told you were wrong for knowing it. Maybe you were a kid and you felt sick before a family gathering. You knew something bad was going to happen. And then it did. And when you tried to tell someone, they said you were being dramatic. Or anxious. Or making things up.

Does that land?

That's the moment the split happens. Your intuition gives you a clear signal. The world around you says that signal is invalid. And because you're a kid who needs to stay safe in your family system, you learn to override it. You learn to doubt the one thing that's actually trying to protect you.

This is what I call the Bright Wound. It's the injury that happens when your sensitivity is met with rejection instead of guidance. When your gifts are treated as problems. When you learn that being perceptive makes you a target instead of making you powerful.

The Bright Wound doesn't make you less intuitive. It makes you afraid of your own intuition. You still feel everything. You just don't trust what you feel. And that's a much harder place to live from.

Healing Starts With Getting Your Signals Straight

Here's the thing nobody tells you about healing: you can't fix what you can't feel. And you can't feel what you've been trained to ignore. Your intuition is the only part of you that kept a record of what actually happened. Not the story you told yourself to survive it. Not the version you edited to make it palatable for other people. The raw, unfiltered truth.

When I work with people on developing their intuition, we always start with the body. Not because I'm a crunchy wellness person - I'm not - but because your body doesn't lie. Your mind will rationalize anything. Your heart will forgive things it shouldn't. But your body? Your body keeps the score.

Intuition isn't some mystical voice from the ether. It's physical information your nervous system is processing faster than your conscious mind can track. It's the tightening in your chest when someone says something that doesn't add up. It's the relaxation in your shoulders when you walk into a room that's safe. It's the nausea that shows up before a decision that's wrong for you.

Healing your intuition means learning to read those signals again. Not as threats. Not as evidence that something is wrong with you. But as data. Clean, useful, life-saving data that your system has been giving you all along.

Step One: Stop Calling It Anxiety

This one is going to piss some people off, and I'm okay with that. Most of what you've been calling anxiety is actually undigested intuitive information. You're not anxious - you're sensing something that needs to be addressed. But because nobody taught you how to process intuitive data, your system just holds it as a low-grade alarm that never turns off.

Think about it. When was the last time you had a "panic attack" that turned out to be completely unfounded? Not a false alarm - I mean a situation where nothing at all was happening and your body just went off for no reason. Almost never, right? Because your intuition is always responding to something real. It might be responding to something from the past that looks like the present. It might be responding to something you can't consciously perceive yet. But it's not random.

Start treating your "anxiety" as information. When you feel that spike, don't try to breathe through it or calm it down. Ask it: what are you trying to tell me? What am I not seeing? What do I already know that I'm pretending not to?

You'll be shocked at what comes through when you stop trying to shut it up.

Step Two: Rebuild the Channel Through the Body

Your intuition got damaged in relationship, and it heals in relationship too. But the first relationship you need to repair is the one with your own physical experience. You can't trust what you feel if you don't know what you're feeling.

Here's a practice that sounds simple but is actually hard: start noticing where in your body you feel things. Not interpreting them. Not judging them. Just noticing. When you walk into a room, do a quick scan. What's happening in your chest? Your stomach? Your throat? Your hands?

Don't change anything. Just observe. Your body has been trying to talk to you for years. It's probably pretty frustrated that you haven't been listening. Let it know you're finally paying attention.

Over time, you'll start to notice patterns. A tight chest means something different from a churning stomach. A heavy feeling in your legs means something different from a buzzing in your hands. Your body has its own vocabulary. Learning that language is the foundation of both intuition and healing.

The Lie About "Letting Go"

I need to address something directly. There's a lot of spiritual teaching that tells you healing means letting go of your wounds. Releasing them. Transcending them. Moving on.

That's a half-truth at best, and it's actively harmful if you apply it too soon.

You can't let go of something you haven't fully held. You can't release something you've never acknowledged. You can't transcend a wound that's still screaming for your attention. The pressure to "let go" before you've truly felt what happened is just another form of spiritual bypass. It's the same message you got as a kid: your experience doesn't matter, get over it, stop being so sensitive.

Your intuition knows this. That's why it keeps bringing things up. Not to torture you. Not because you're broken. But because it's waiting for you to finally listen. To finally say, "I see that. I feel that. That happened to me and it mattered."

Healing isn't about getting rid of your wounds. It's about integrating them. Making them part of your story instead of the whole story. And your intuition is the only tool that can show you how to do that without getting lost in the pain.

If you're looking for a practical guide to start this process, I recommend The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not a spiritual book - it's a science book. But it will show you exactly how your body holds what your mind can't process, and why listening to physical sensation is the way through.

How Intuition Changes the Healing Timeline

Most people approach healing like it's a linear process. First you acknowledge the wound. Then you feel the feelings. Then you do the work. Then you're healed. Like a checklist. Like a to-do list.

That's not how it works. Healing is spiral. You come back to the same wound at different levels, with more capacity, more understanding, more integration. And your intuition is what tells you which level you're at.

Your intuition will let you know when you're ready to touch something you couldn't touch before. It will let you know when you're avoiding something you need to face. It will let you know when you've done enough and it's time to rest.

The problem is, most people don't trust that. They push when they should rest. They rest when they should push. They take advice from people who don't know their history instead of listening to their own system.

Your intuition is the only guide that knows your exact timeline. Not your therapist's timeline. Not your spiritual teacher's timeline. Not what you read in a book. Your timeline. The one that respects how much you've been through and how far you've already come.

Learning to trust that timeline is healing in itself. Every time you listen to your gut and honor what it says, you're repairing the Bright Wound. You're telling yourself, "I believe you. I trust you. You were right all along."

For a deeper exploration of how to work with your inner guidance system, The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown is worth your time. It's about shame and vulnerability, but underneath that, it's about learning to trust your own knowing enough to show up as you actually are.

The Practical Work: Listening While It Hurts

Let me give you something you can actually use. Because I know you're tired of theory. You want to know what to do when you're in the middle of it and your intuition is screaming and your wounds are open and you don't know which way is up.

When You're Activated

First, stop trying to figure out if what you're feeling is "real" or "just your trauma." That question is a trap. It's a way for your mind to avoid the actual experience. Instead, ask a different question: "What is this sensation trying to tell me about what I need right now?"

If you're triggered, your intuition might be telling you that you're not safe. Not that you're in danger - but that your nervous system is detecting a pattern that was unsafe before. Honor that. Don't argue with it. Say, "I hear you. I know this feels familiar. We're not in that situation anymore, but I understand why you're sounding the alarm."

That conversation alone changes everything. You're not fighting your system anymore. You're partnering with it.

When You're Numb

Numbness is also information. It means your system has gone into protection mode because it couldn't handle any more input. Don't try to force feeling. Don't try to "open up." That's like trying to force someone to trust you by yelling at them.

Instead, just acknowledge the numbness. Say, "I know there's something here that I can't feel right now. I trust that I'll feel it when I'm ready." Then go about your day. Your intuition is still working, even when you can't feel it. It's managing the pace of what you can handle.

When You're Confused

Confusion is usually a sign that you're trying to figure something out with your mind that can only be known through your body. Stop thinking. Do something physical. Walk. Stretch. Shake your hands out. Take a shower. Let your body process while your mind takes a break.

I can't tell you how many times I've gotten clarity in the shower or on a walk that I couldn't get sitting at my desk trying to "figure it out." Your intuition works better when you're not trying to control it.

And if you want a structured way to work with your body's wisdom, The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle is a classic for a reason. It's about presence, but presence is just another word for being in your body enough to hear what it's telling you.

What Healing Actually Looks Like

Let me paint you a picture of what this looks like when it's working. Not the end state - I don't believe in end states. But the process, when it's moving.

You start to notice that you trust yourself more. Not in a big, dramatic way. In small ways. You choose the restaurant you actually want instead of the one everyone else wants. You say no to a social event without explaining yourself. You leave a conversation that feels off instead of staying and trying to fix it.

You start to notice that your body relaxes more often. Not because you've meditated it into submission, but because your system finally believes that you're listening. That you'll respond when it signals. That it doesn't have to scream to get your attention.

You start to notice that your intuition gets clearer. Not louder - clearer. Because you're not filtering it through fear anymore. You're not second-guessing every signal. You're receiving the information and acting on it, and that feedback loop strengthens the connection.

And the wounds? They don't disappear. But they stop running the show. They become part of your world instead of the whole territory. You can touch them without falling into them. You can talk about them without getting lost in them. They're there, they matter, but they're not who you are.

That's the healing that intuition makes possible. Not transcendence. Integration. Not escape. Wholeness.

The Only Question That Matters

I want to leave you with something to sit with. Not an answer. A question. The question that underlies all of this work.

What would it mean to trust yourself completely?

Not conditionally. Not when you've checked with five other people first. Not when you have proof that you're right. Completely. To believe that the thing you've always known is true, even when nobody else sees it. To act on your own knowing before you have permission. To let your intuition lead instead of following someone else's map.

That's the healing. That's the destination. And it's not somewhere you arrive - it's something you practice every single day.

You already have everything you need. You always did. The Bright Wound taught you to doubt that. But the wound is not your identity. It's just the place where your light got hidden. And the same sensitivity that made you a target is the thing that's going to show you the way home.

If this landed for you, and you're ready to go deeper into the work of trusting your own knowing, I've put together something for you. It's a short assessment that helps you identify where you're at in the process of rebuilding your intuitive trust. No fluff. No sales pitch. Just a tool to help you see where you are and what's next. You can take it here.
